A1.definition: A round fruit with red or green skin
examples: I eat an apple every day.
She bought red apples from the store.
Apple juice is my favorite drink.

A1.definition: A written or printed work consisting of pages
examples: I read a book before bed.
This book is very interesting.
She gave me a book for my birthday.

A1.definition: A small domesticated carnivorous mammal
examples: The cat is sleeping on the sofa.
My cat loves playing with yarn.
We adopted a cat from the shelter.

A1.definition: A domesticated carnivorous mammal
examples: The dog is playing in the garden.
My dog likes to fetch the ball.
She walks her dog every morning.

A1.definition: To put food into the mouth and chew and swallow it
examples: I eat breakfast at 8 o'clock.
We eat dinner together every evening.
He likes to eat pizza.

A1.definition: A group of people related to each other
examples: My family is very big.
We visit our family on holidays.
Family is important to me.

A1.definition: Of a high quality or standard
examples: This is a very good movie.
She is a good student.
The food tastes good.

A1.definition: A building for people to live in
examples: We live in a big house.
The house has three bedrooms.
They bought a new house last year.

A1.definition: A clear liquid with no color or taste
examples: I drink a lot of water.
The water is very cold.
Can I have a glass of water?

A1.definition: Feeling or showing pleasure
examples: I am very happy today.
She looks happy in the photo.
The children are happy playing outside.

A1.definition: A place where children go to learn
examples: I go to school every day.
My school is near my house.
School starts at nine o'clock.

A1.definition: A person you know well and like
examples: She is my best friend.
I made a new friend today.
Friends help each other.

A1.definition: Of considerable size or extent
examples: That is a big tree.
He has a big smile.
We need a big table for dinner.

A1.definition: Of a size that is less than normal
examples: The box is very small.
She has a small dog.
We live in a small town.

A1.definition: To move using your legs faster than walking
examples: I run every morning.
The children run in the park.
He can run very fast.

A1.definition: Activity involving effort done to achieve a result
examples: I work in an office.
She works very hard.
We work from Monday to Friday.

A1.definition: A road vehicle with an engine and four wheels
examples: He drives a red car.
The car is parked outside.
We went by car to the beach.

A1.definition: The indefinite continued progress of existence
examples: What time is it?
I don't have time today.
Time flies when you're having fun.

A1.definition: Recently made or discovered
examples: I bought a new phone.
She started a new job.
This is a new restaurant.

A1.definition: Having lived for a long time
examples: My grandmother is very old.
This is an old building.
He sold his old car.

A1.definition: A female parent
examples: My mother makes great food.
She became a mother last year.
I call my mother every day.

A1.definition: A male parent
examples: My father works in a hospital.
He is a good father.
Father and son went fishing.

A1.definition: Pleasing the senses or mind aesthetically
examples: What a beautiful day!
She has a beautiful voice.
The garden is beautiful in spring.

A1.definition: To make it easier for someone to do something
examples: Can you help me please?
She always helps her friends.
I need help with my homework.

A1.definition: To move so as to allow access
examples: Please open the door.
She opened her book.
The shop opens at nine.

A1.definition: To move so as to cover an opening
examples: Please close the window.
He closed his eyes.
The store closes at eight.

A1.definition: Vocal or instrumental sounds combined
examples: I love listening to music.
She plays beautiful music.
Music makes me happy.

A1.definition: Any substance consumed to provide nutrition
examples: The food here is delicious.
We need to buy food.
Italian food is my favorite.

A1.definition: A young human being
examples: The child is playing outside.
Every child needs love.
She is a happy child.

A1.definition: Of or at a low temperature
examples: It is very cold today.
I drink cold water.
She feels cold in winter.

A1.definition: Having a high temperature
examples: The coffee is too hot.
It is very hot in summer.
Be careful, the plate is hot.

A1.definition: A condition of body and mind in which the nervous system is inactive
examples: I sleep eight hours a night.
The baby is sleeping.
I couldn't sleep last night.

A1.definition: The period of time between midnight and noon
examples: Good morning!
I exercise every morning.
The mornings are cold in winter.

A1.definition: The period of darkness between sunset and sunrise
examples: Good night!
The stars are bright at night.
I study at night.

A1.definition: The property of producing different sensations on the eye
examples: What is your favorite color?
The sky is blue in color.
She painted the room a warm color.

A2.definition: To accept that something is true
examples: I believe you are right.
She believes in hard work.
Do you believe this story?

A2.definition: To go from one place to another
examples: I love to travel.
She travels for work.
We traveled to Japan last summer.

A2.definition: To have in or be able to bring to one's mind
examples: I remember my first day at school.
Please remember to call me.
She remembers everything.

A2.definition: Not the same as another
examples: We have different opinions.
Each person is different.
This cake tastes different.

A2.definition: Of great significance or value
examples: Education is very important.
This is an important meeting.
It's important to eat healthy.

A2.definition: Occasionally, rather than all of the time
examples: I sometimes go for a walk.
She sometimes forgets her keys.
Sometimes it rains in summer.

A2.definition: In or along the space separating two objects
examples: The park is between the school and the library.
Choose between these two options.
There is a wall between the houses.

A2.definition: To make sounds and movements of the face
examples: She always makes me laugh.
The children laughed at the clown.
Don't laugh at him.

A2.definition: Providing physical ease and relaxation
examples: This chair is very comfortable.
She feels comfortable at home.
Wear comfortable shoes for the trip.

A2.definition: Practical contact with events or activities
examples: She has a lot of experience.
It was a great experience.
Work experience is very important.

A2.definition: To perceive the intended meaning
examples: I understand the problem.
Do you understand me?
She understands three languages.

A2.definition: Able or likely to cause harm or injury
examples: Swimming here is dangerous.
That is a dangerous road.
Some animals are dangerous.

A2.definition: To take place or occur
examples: What happened yesterday?
Accidents can happen anytime.
Something strange happened to me.

A2.definition: To make or become better
examples: I want to improve my English.
The weather will improve tomorrow.
She improved her grades.

A2.definition: Throughout the course of
examples: I fell asleep during the movie.
She works during the day.
During the summer, we go swimming.

A2.definition: Arousing curiosity or interest
examples: This is an interesting book.
She told me an interesting story.
The museum was very interesting.

A2.definition: The state of the atmosphere at a place
examples: The weather is nice today.
What's the weather like?
Bad weather is expected tomorrow.

A2.definition: As much or as many as required
examples: We have enough food.
Is this big enough?
She didn't study enough.

A2.definition: To perform an activity repeatedly to improve
examples: Practice makes perfect.
She practices piano every day.
I need to practice my speaking.

A2.definition: An action or judgment that is wrong
examples: Everyone makes mistakes.
I made a mistake on the test.
Learn from your mistakes.

A2.definition: A system of communication used by a country
examples: She speaks three languages.
English is a global language.
Learning a language takes time.

A2.definition: To make something clear or easy to understand
examples: Can you explain this to me?
She explained the rules clearly.
Let me explain the situation.

A2.definition: Very enthusiastic and eager
examples: I'm excited about the trip.
The children are excited.
She was excited to see her friends.

A2.definition: To make ready for use or consideration
examples: She prepared dinner for us.
I need to prepare for the exam.
Prepare your documents in advance.

A2.definition: To grow or cause to grow more advanced
examples: She wants to develop her skills.
The city has developed rapidly.
We need to develop a new plan.

A2.definition: Used to express uncertainty or possibility
examples: Perhaps we should wait.
Perhaps it will rain later.
She is perhaps the best singer here.

A2.definition: In or to a foreign country
examples: She studied abroad for a year.
Have you ever been abroad?
Many students want to go abroad.

A2.definition: Able to be used or obtained
examples: Is this seat available?
She is not available right now.
Tickets are available online.

A2.definition: An act of selecting between alternatives
examples: You have three choices.
It was a difficult choice.
She made the right choice.

A2.definition: Liked or admired by many people
examples: This song is very popular.
She is popular at school.
Pizza is a popular food worldwide.

A2.definition: An informal talk between people
examples: We had an interesting conversation.
She started a conversation with him.
Good conversations make friendships stronger.

A2.definition: In spite of the fact that
examples: Although it rained, we went out.
She smiled although she was tired.
Although he is young, he is mature.

A2.definition: An unusual and exciting experience
examples: Life is an adventure.
She loves adventure sports.
Our trip was a great adventure.

A2.definition: Easily agitated or apprehensive
examples: I feel nervous before exams.
She was nervous about the interview.
Don't be nervous, you'll do great.

A2.definition: Not long ago
examples: I recently started a new job.
She recently moved to London.
Have you traveled recently?

B1.definition: To successfully reach a desired objective
examples: She achieved her goals through hard work.
It takes dedication to achieve success.
He achieved the highest score in class.

B1.definition: A condition giving a greater chance of success
examples: Being bilingual is a great advantage.
We should take advantage of this opportunity.
He has an advantage over other candidates.

B1.definition: To focus all attention on a particular activity
examples: I can't concentrate with this noise.
She concentrated on her studies.
You need to concentrate harder.

B1.definition: An event in which people compete
examples: She won the competition easily.
There is a lot of competition for the job.
The competition starts next week.

B1.definition: Without any doubt
examples: I will definitely be there.
She is definitely the best candidate.
This is definitely worth trying.

B1.definition: Sad because something was not as good as expected
examples: I was disappointed with the result.
She felt disappointed after the interview.
Don't be disappointed, try again.

B1.definition: To give support, confidence, or hope
examples: Teachers should encourage their students.
She encouraged me to apply for the job.
We need to encourage healthy habits.

B1.definition: The surroundings or conditions in which a person lives
examples: We must protect the environment.
The work environment is very positive.
Changes in the environment affect wildlife.

B1.definition: In the end
examples: She eventually found her keys.
Eventually, he agreed with us.
I will eventually learn to cook well.

B1.definition: Regularly or habitually; often
examples: She frequently visits her parents.
Buses run frequently during the day.
I frequently forget my password.

B1.definition: Willing to give more of something
examples: He is very generous with his time.
She made a generous donation.
They are generous people.

B1.definition: In a gradual way; slowly
examples: The weather gradually improved.
She gradually got better at chess.
Prices have gradually increased.

B1.definition: To pause before doing something
examples: Don't hesitate to ask for help.
She hesitated before answering.
He never hesitates to share his opinion.

B1.definition: The capacity to have an effect on character or behavior
examples: Music has a big influence on my mood.
She is a positive influence on her team.
Social media has a huge influence on teenagers.

B1.definition: A set of circumstances making something possible
examples: This is a great opportunity for growth.
Don't miss this opportunity.
She was given the opportunity to study abroad.

B1.definition: With no special or distinctive features
examples: It was just an ordinary day.
She is no ordinary teacher.
He lives an ordinary life.

B1.definition: Able to accept delays without becoming annoyed
examples: Please be patient with me.
Good teachers are patient.
She waited patiently for her turn.

B1.definition: To cause someone to do something through reasoning
examples: I tried to persuade him to stay.
She persuaded me to try the food.
It's hard to persuade him to change his mind.

B1.definition: Of great value; not to be wasted
examples: Time is precious.
Water is a precious resource.
She keeps her precious memories alive.

B1.definition: Almost certainly; very likely
examples: It will probably rain tomorrow.
She is probably at home.
We will probably arrive late.

B1.definition: The state of being responsible for something
examples: It's your responsibility to finish it.
She takes her responsibilities seriously.
Parents have a big responsibility.

B1.definition: To fulfill the expectations or desires of
examples: Nothing seems to satisfy him.
The meal satisfied everyone.
She needs to satisfy the requirements.

B1.definition: A set of circumstances in which one finds oneself
examples: It's a difficult situation.
We need to analyze the situation.
She handled the situation well.

B1.definition: A means of solving a problem
examples: We need to find a solution quickly.
There is no easy solution.
She proposed a creative solution.

B1.definition: To make forceful efforts to get free
examples: Many students struggle with math.
She struggled to stay awake.
He struggled against the disease.

B1.definition: To achieve the desired aim or result
examples: She succeeded in passing the exam.
If you try hard, you will succeed.
He wants to succeed in business.

B1.definition: To put forward for consideration
examples: I suggest we leave early.
She suggested a new approach.
Can you suggest a good restaurant?

B1.definition: Having the distinctive qualities of a type
examples: This is a typical Turkish breakfast.
It's typical weather for this season.
She is a typical example of a leader.

B1.definition: Worth a great deal of money or importance
examples: This is very valuable information.
Time is the most valuable resource.
She gave me valuable advice.

B1.definition: The quality of being different or diverse
examples: The store offers a wide variety of products.
She enjoys a variety of music.
Variety is the spice of life.

B1.definition: To desire or be curious to know something
examples: I wonder what happened.
She wondered about the meaning of life.
I often wonder why he left.

B1.definition: To identify someone or something from having encountered them before
examples: I didn't recognize her at first.
He recognized the song immediately.
She recognized the importance of education.

B1.definition: To say or estimate that something will happen in the future
examples: It's hard to predict the weather.
Experts predict a rise in temperatures.
She predicted the outcome correctly.

B1.definition: An insistent and peremptory request
examples: The demand for electric cars is growing.
She met all the demands of the job.
Supply must meet demand.

B1.definition: Successful in producing a desired result
examples: This is a very effective method.
She is an effective communicator.
The treatment was effective.

B2.definition: To accept or admit the existence or truth of
examples: He acknowledged his mistake publicly.
She acknowledged the importance of teamwork.
It's important to acknowledge your feelings.

B2.definition: Open to more than one interpretation
examples: The statement was deliberately ambiguous.
His instructions were ambiguous.
Avoid ambiguous language in contracts.

B2.definition: A result or effect of an action
examples: Every action has a consequence.
She faced the consequences of her decision.
The consequences could be severe.

B2.definition: To think deeply and at length about something
examples: She contemplated her future carefully.
He sat and contemplated the view.
I'm contemplating a career change.

B2.definition: Giving rise to public disagreement
examples: It was a controversial decision.
The topic is quite controversial.
She made a controversial statement.

B2.definition: Based on what is traditionally done
examples: He chose a conventional career path.
Conventional methods are not always best.
She prefers conventional cooking.

B2.definition: Of great importance; critical
examples: This is a crucial moment in history.
Timing is crucial in business.
She played a crucial role in the project.

B2.definition: Done consciously and intentionally
examples: It was a deliberate act of kindness.
She made a deliberate effort to improve.
The decision was deliberate, not accidental.

B2.definition: A situation requiring a difficult choice
examples: She faced a moral dilemma.
It's a common dilemma for parents.
He was caught in a dilemma.

B2.definition: A difference between similar things
examples: There is a clear distinction between the two.
She graduated with distinction.
The distinction is subtle but important.

B2.definition: Involving careful detail and complexity
examples: She prepared an elaborate presentation.
The plan was quite elaborate.
He gave an elaborate explanation.

B2.definition: To intensify, increase, or further improve
examples: Music can enhance your mood.
Technology enhances our daily lives.
She enhanced the design with new colors.

B2.definition: To use a situation for one's advantage
examples: Companies exploit natural resources.
She exploited the opportunity fully.
Don't exploit people's kindness.

B2.definition: Forming a necessary base or core
examples: Respect is fundamental in any relationship.
This is a fundamental change in policy.
She has a fundamental understanding of physics.

B2.definition: Truly what something is said to be; authentic
examples: She expressed genuine concern.
This is a genuine leather bag.
His smile was genuine.

B2.definition: A proposed explanation for a phenomenon
examples: The scientist tested her hypothesis.
This hypothesis needs more evidence.
He formed a hypothesis about the problem.

B2.definition: Certain to happen; unavoidable
examples: Change is inevitable.
The delay was inevitable due to the storm.
It was inevitable that she would succeed.

B2.definition: An accurate and deep understanding
examples: The book provides valuable insight into human behavior.
She gained insight from the experience.
His insights were incredibly helpful.

B2.definition: To show or prove to be right or reasonable
examples: How can you justify this decision?
She tried to justify her actions.
The results justified the effort.

B2.definition: Conforming to the law or to rules
examples: She has a legitimate reason for being absent.
Is this a legitimate business?
His concerns are legitimate.

B2.definition: To control or influence something cleverly
examples: He tried to manipulate the results.
Don't let anyone manipulate you.
She can manipulate data effectively.

B2.definition: In spite of that; notwithstanding
examples: It was raining; nevertheless, we went out.
She was tired; nevertheless, she kept working.
The task was difficult. Nevertheless, they succeeded.

B2.definition: To become aware of through the senses
examples: She perceived a change in his attitude.
People perceive risk differently.
He is perceived as a strong leader.

B2.definition: A fact or situation that is observed to exist
examples: Global warming is a well-known phenomenon.
Social media is a modern phenomenon.
It's a natural phenomenon.

B2.definition: In exact terms; without vagueness
examples: That is precisely what I meant.
She arrived precisely on time.
Measure the ingredients precisely.

B2.definition: Unwilling and hesitant
examples: She was reluctant to share her opinion.
He is reluctant to make changes.
They were reluctant participants.

B2.definition: Sufficiently great or important to be worthy of attention
examples: This is a significant achievement.
There was a significant increase in sales.
She played a significant role.

B2.definition: Having a refined knowledge of the world
examples: She has sophisticated taste in art.
The software is highly sophisticated.
He gave a sophisticated argument.

B2.definition: Of considerable importance, size, or worth
examples: She received a substantial pay raise.
There is substantial evidence to support this.
He made a substantial contribution.

B2.definition: An inclination toward a particular characteristic
examples: He has a tendency to exaggerate.
There is a growing tendency toward remote work.
She has a tendency to overthink.

B2.definition: In a complete and detailed manner
examples: She thoroughly enjoyed the concert.
Clean the kitchen thoroughly.
He researched the topic thoroughly.

B2.definition: Significant but not immediately obvious
examples: What is the underlying cause?
There is an underlying theme throughout the book.
She addressed the underlying issues.

B2.definition: Found or distributed over a large area
examples: There is widespread concern about pollution.
The disease became widespread.
The practice is widespread in Europe.

B2.definition: To remove or take away
examples: She decided to withdraw from the competition.
He withdrew money from the bank.
The army was forced to withdraw.

B2.definition: To produce or provide a natural or industrial product
examples: The investment yielded good returns.
These trees yield excellent fruit.
The experiment yielded unexpected results.

C1.definition: To formally put an end to a system or practice
examples: The government abolished the old tax law.
Many countries have abolished the death penalty.
They seek to abolish unfair labor practices.

C1.definition: Existing in thought or as an idea without concrete existence
examples: The concept is too abstract for children.
She paints in an abstract style.
Abstract thinking is essential for philosophy.

C1.definition: To gather together or acquire an increasing amount
examples: She accumulated a lot of knowledge over the years.
Dust tends to accumulate on shelves.
He accumulated wealth through wise investments.

C1.definition: To publicly recommend or support
examples: She advocates for human rights.
He advocates a healthy lifestyle.
Many experts advocate for renewable energy.

C1.definition: A claim that someone has done something wrong
examples: The allegations were never proven.
She denied all the allegations.
Serious allegations were made against the company.

C1.definition: To make suffering less severe
examples: Medicine can alleviate pain.
The government took steps to alleviate poverty.
She tried to alleviate his concerns.

C1.definition: The quality of being open to more than one interpretation
examples: The contract was full of ambiguity.
There was no ambiguity in her response.
Legal documents should avoid ambiguity.

C1.definition: To regard as probable; expect or predict
examples: I didn't anticipate this problem.
She anticipated the question and prepared an answer.
We anticipate strong demand for the product.

C1.definition: Logical and consistent
examples: She gave a coherent explanation.
The plan needs to be more coherent.
His argument was clear and coherent.

C1.definition: The act of obeying a rule or request
examples: Compliance with the law is mandatory.
She ensured compliance with safety regulations.
The company is in full compliance.

C1.definition: Including all or nearly all elements or aspects
examples: She wrote a comprehensive report.
We need a comprehensive plan.
The exam was comprehensive and challenging.

C1.definition: To form or devise a plan or idea
examples: She conceived the idea during college.
It's hard to conceive of a world without technology.
He conceived a brilliant strategy.

C1.definition: To keep within certain limits
examples: She tried to confine her remarks to the topic.
The disease was confined to one region.
Don't confine yourself to one approach.

C1.definition: To make something physically stronger or more solid
examples: She consolidated her position as leader.
The companies decided to consolidate.
He needs to consolidate his skills.

C1.definition: The feeling that a person or thing is worthless
examples: She looked at him with contempt.
He showed contempt for the rules.
Contempt can destroy relationships.

C1.definition: To deny the truth of a statement by asserting the opposite
examples: The evidence contradicts his testimony.
Don't contradict yourself in the interview.
Her actions contradict her words.

C1.definition: To represent or show something in an image or story
examples: The painting depicts a rural landscape.
The novel depicts life in the 1920s.
She depicted the scene with great detail.

C1.definition: To become progressively worse
examples: His health deteriorated rapidly.
The situation continued to deteriorate.
Buildings deteriorate without maintenance.

C1.definition: The quality of behaving in a careful and controlled way
examples: Use your own discretion in this matter.
She handled the situation with great discretion.
The judge used her discretion wisely.

C1.definition: A person's inherent qualities of mind and character
examples: She has a cheerful disposition.
His calm disposition makes him a good leader.
People with a positive disposition live longer.

C1.definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing
examples: She gave an eloquent speech.
He is an eloquent writer.
Her silence was more eloquent than words.

C1.definition: To surround and have or hold within
examples: The course encompasses all major topics.
Her research encompasses several fields.
The park encompasses 500 acres.

C1.definition: An attempt to achieve a goal
examples: Scientific endeavor has improved our lives.
She put great endeavor into her work.
Every creative endeavor starts with an idea.

C1.definition: To gradually destroy or be gradually destroyed
examples: Wind and rain erode the rocks.
Trust can erode quickly.
His confidence eroded over time.

C1.definition: To represent something as being larger or more important
examples: He tends to exaggerate his achievements.
Don't exaggerate the problem.
She exaggerated the difficulty of the task.

C1.definition: A short extract from a film, broadcast, or text
examples: She read an excerpt from the novel.
Here is an excerpt from his speech.
The excerpt was taken from chapter three.

C1.definition: Relating to government revenue and taxation
examples: The government announced new fiscal policies.
The fiscal year ends in March.
She works in fiscal planning.

C1.definition: To grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way
examples: The business flourished under her leadership.
Plants flourish in warm climates.
Creativity flourishes in a supportive environment.

C1.definition: Inspiring fear or respect through being impressively large or powerful
examples: She is a formidable opponent.
The task seemed formidable at first.
He built a formidable reputation.

C1.definition: Incapable of producing any useful result; pointless
examples: Resistance was futile.
It's futile to argue with him.
Their efforts proved futile.

C1.definition: About to happen
examples: A storm is imminent.
She sensed that danger was imminent.
The announcement is imminent.

C1.definition: The conclusion that can be drawn from something
examples: What are the implications of this decision?
The implications are far-reaching.
She didn't consider the implications.

C1.definition: Without intention; accidentally
examples: She inadvertently deleted the file.
He inadvertently revealed the secret.
The error was made inadvertently.

C1.definition: The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
examples: She is a person of great integrity.
He questioned the integrity of the research.
Integrity is essential in leadership.

C1.definition: Producing a great deal of profit
examples: She found a lucrative career in finance.
Real estate can be very lucrative.
That's a lucrative business opportunity.

C2.definition: To become less intense or widespread
examples: The storm showed no signs of abating.
Public anger failed to abate.
The noise finally abated after midnight.

C2.definition: A departure from what is normal or expected
examples: The warm winter was an aberration.
This behavior is an aberration from his usual conduct.
Statistical aberrations can mislead researchers.

C2.definition: Angry and bitter
examples: The divorce was acrimonious.
An acrimonious debate erupted in parliament.
She wanted to avoid an acrimonious discussion.

C2.definition: To combine or unite to form one structure
examples: The two companies amalgamated last year.
They decided to amalgamate their resources.
Several departments were amalgamated.

C2.definition: A thing belonging to a period other than that in which it exists
examples: The use of typewriters is an anachronism.
The monarchy is viewed by some as an anachronism.
Historical films often contain anachronisms.

C2.definition: Hostile and aggressive
examples: He became belligerent when challenged.
The belligerent nations refused to negotiate.
Her belligerent tone shocked everyone.

C2.definition: Concise and exact use of words
examples: Brevity is the soul of wit.
He was known for the brevity of his speeches.
The brevity of life makes every moment precious.

C2.definition: Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood
examples: The weather here is capricious.
She is known for her capricious behavior.
Capricious leadership creates instability.

C2.definition: To find a way around an obstacle
examples: They tried to circumvent the regulations.
She circumvented the problem by using a different method.
Companies often circumvent tax laws.

C2.definition: Kept secret or done secretively
examples: They held clandestine meetings at night.
The clandestine operation was discovered.
She led a clandestine life.

C2.definition: A confusing and difficult problem or question
examples: The budget deficit is a conundrum.
It's a moral conundrum with no easy answer.
She faced the conundrum of choosing between two jobs.

C2.definition: To make someone very weak
examples: The disease debilitated him for months.
Chronic stress can debilitate the immune system.
The injury debilitated the athlete.

C2.definition: To describe or portray something precisely
examples: She delineated the boundaries of the project.
The report clearly delineates the risks involved.
It's important to delineate roles and responsibilities.

C2.definition: Cheerful and full of energy
examples: She was in an ebullient mood after the promotion.
His ebullient personality lights up every room.
The crowd was ebullient after the victory.

C2.definition: Lasting for a very short time
examples: Social media fame is often ephemeral.
The beauty of cherry blossoms is ephemeral.
Ephemeral trends come and go quickly.

C2.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous
examples: His response was deliberately equivocal.
The results of the study were equivocal.
She gave an equivocal answer to avoid commitment.

C2.definition: To make a problem or situation worse
examples: The drought exacerbated the food crisis.
Stress can exacerbate health problems.
His comments only exacerbated the tension.

C2.definition: Very attentive to detail and accuracy
examples: She is fastidious about her work.
He kept his house in fastidious order.
The editor was fastidious about grammar.

C2.definition: Fond of company; sociable
examples: She has a gregarious personality.
He is naturally gregarious and makes friends easily.
Gregarious people thrive in social settings.

C2.definition: Relating to a distinctive individual characteristic
examples: She has an idiosyncratic sense of humor.
His writing style is highly idiosyncratic.
Each culture has its own idiosyncratic customs.

C2.definition: Not allowing fluid to pass through; unable to be affected
examples: She seemed impervious to criticism.
The material is impervious to water.
He remained impervious to their pleas.

C2.definition: Proceeding in a gradual and harmful way
examples: Misinformation has an insidious effect on society.
The disease is particularly insidious.
He warned of the insidious influence of propaganda.

C2.definition: To place close together for contrasting effect
examples: The artist juxtaposed light and dark colors.
The documentary juxtaposes wealth and poverty.
She juxtaposed modern and traditional designs.

C2.definition: Very generous or forgiving
examples: She was magnanimous in victory.
He made a magnanimous gesture toward his rival.
A truly magnanimous leader inspires loyalty.

C2.definition: Typically of an action or activity, wicked or criminal
examples: The villain had nefarious plans.
She uncovered a nefarious scheme.
His nefarious activities were finally exposed.

C2.definition: To make something unclear or unintelligible
examples: Politicians sometimes obfuscate the truth.
The jargon only serves to obfuscate the issue.
Don't try to obfuscate the facts.

C2.definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way
examples: Racism is a pernicious evil.
The pernicious effects of pollution are well documented.
She warned of the pernicious influence of social media.

C2.definition: Representing the most perfect or typical example
examples: She is the quintessential professional.
This is the quintessential British novel.
He embodies the quintessential spirit of entrepreneurship.

C2.definition: Having an obstinately uncooperative attitude
examples: The recalcitrant student refused to follow the rules.
They dealt with the recalcitrant nation firmly.
She was recalcitrant in the face of authority.

C2.definition: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved
examples: She took a surreptitious glance at her phone.
He made surreptitious payments to the official.
Their surreptitious meetings went unnoticed.

C2.definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere
examples: Smartphones are ubiquitous in modern life.
Coffee shops have become ubiquitous in the city.
The brand has become ubiquitous worldwide.

C2.definition: Experienced in the imagination through the feelings of another
examples: She lived vicariously through her children.
He got vicarious pleasure from watching travel shows.
Reading offers vicarious experiences.

C2.definition: Occupying much space; very lengthy and detailed
examples: She wore a voluminous gown.
He produced voluminous notes during research.
The voluminous report covered every detail.

C2.definition: Having great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause
examples: She is a zealous advocate for education.
He was zealous in his efforts to reform the system.
The zealous fans cheered all night.

C2.definition: A trace of something that is disappearing or no longer exists
examples: Not a vestige of the old building remained.
The law is a vestige of a bygone era.
She found vestiges of the ancient civilization.
